nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, soft, acidic blackwater rivers and flooded forest margins; heavily vegetated areas with submerged and emergent plant growth

decorPreferences: driftwood and roots for cover and visual breaks
dim lighting with floating plants (e.g., water lettuce, Amazon frogbit) to reduce skittishness
artificial plants or robust species such as Java fern, Anubias, or Bolbitis — live plants will be eaten
open mid-water swimming space for shoaling

diet.notes: Dedicated herbivore that consumes plant matter ravenously — will rapidly destroy a planted aquarium. Primary diet: spirulina flakes, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, romaine lettuce, peas, spinach), vegetable-based pellets. Also accepts fruit (melon, banana pieces) and aquatic plants. Supplement with occasional treats such as freeze-dried spirulina or high-quality colour-enhancing flakes. Will nibble at most soft-leaved plants in the tank; only very tough-leaved plants (Java fern, Anubias, large Amazon swords briefly) may survive.

goodWith: large peaceful characins (e.g., Congo tetras, larger tetra species)
medium-to-large peaceful cichlids (e.g., oscars, severums, geophagus) provided the cichlids are not overly aggressive
large peaceful barbs (e.g., tinfoil barbs, spanner barbs)
large plecos and catfish (e.g., common pleco, royal pleco)
avoidWith: live aquatic plants — will be consumed
very small fish (tetras, rasboras, nano species) — risk of being intimidated or accidentally outcompeted at feeding
overly boisterous or aggressive tankmates that may cause skittish bolting and injury

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo page for Metynnis argenteus (ID 4163). Multiple contributor images available; verify individual image licenses before use.
Wikimedia Commons category for Metynnis argenteus. Check specific file license on the detail page.

summary: The Silver Dollar is a large, disc-shaped characin from South American blackwater rivers. Peaceful and shoaling, it belongs to the piranha family but is a dedicated herbivore that will devastate a planted aquarium. Kept in groups of 6+ in tanks of 300 L or more.
careNotes: Silver Dollars (Metynnis argenteus) are large, laterally compressed characins whose bright, mirror-like flanks give them their common name. Despite membership in the family Serrasalmidae — the piranha family — they are entirely peaceful herbivores. Their plant-eating habit is the single most important care consideration: Silver Dollars will consume virtually all soft-leaved aquatic plants within days. Only very tough-leaved species such as Java fern (Microsorum pteropus), Anubias, or Bolbitis have a reasonable chance of surviving, and even these may be nibbled. Artificial plants or a bare/driftwood-only hardscape is strongly recommended. Keep in shoals of at least 6 individuals; smaller groups produce noticeably skittish behaviour, with fish prone to panicked bolting that can result in impact injuries or escape attempts. A tight-fitting lid is essential — Silver Dollars are strong jumpers. Dim lighting, floating plant cover, and plenty of visual structure (driftwood, rocks) help reduce stress. Water should be warm (24–28°C), soft to moderately hard (gH 2–15), and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.5), matching their Amazonian blackwater origins. Regular water changes of 25–30% weekly are important given the large bioload of a 6+ fish group. Often confused with the Spotted Silver Dollar (Metynnis lippincottianus), which has small dark spots across the flanks, and the Red-Hook Silver Dollar (Myloplus rubripinnis, formerly Myleus rubripinnis), which has a distinctive red anal fin. Confirm genus and species before purchase if a specific species is desired. Note: no eatsPlants boolean field exists in the schema; plant destruction is addressed in diet.notes and here in careNotes.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers that breed in the mid-water column or over plants. A large, species-specific breeding tank (400 L+) with dim lighting, soft acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–4, temperature 27–28°C), and fine-leaved plants or spawning mops is recommended. A well-conditioned pair or group will spawn seasonally, scattering up to 2,000 adhesive eggs among plants. Eggs hatch in approximately 3 days and fry become free-swimming after a further 4–5 days. Remove adults after spawning as eggs and fry may be eaten. First foods: infusoria, micro-worms, then finely powdered spirulina flake and baby brine shrimp as fry grow. Breeding in captivity is possible but not commonly attempted in home aquaria due to the tank size required.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Metynnis argenteus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, water parameters (pH 5.5–7.5, temperature 24–28°C, hardness 36–179 ppm/2–10 dH), native range (Tapajós and Paraguay river basins, northern South America), diet (herbivore), shoaling requirement (6+), breeding notes, and tankmate guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Metynnis argenteus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 14 cm TL per FishBase, extended to 15 cm per broader literature and hobbyist reports), taxonomy (Serrasalmidae, Characiformes), native range (Tapajós, upper Paraguay basins; Brazil, Bolivia, Argentina), and diet (herbivore, primarily plant material). FishBase lists max TL ~14 cm; 15 cm used as practical aquarium maximum consistent with SeriouslyFish and hobby sources.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Silver Dollar Fish Care
additional.notes: Confirmed herbivore diet, plant-destruction warning, shoaling (6+) requirement, tank size recommendation (75–100 gal / ~285–380 L), escape risk, and peaceful temperament. Also noted common confusion with M. lippincottianus (spotted) and Myloplus rubripinnis (red hook).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Silver Dollar
additional.notes: Confirmed Serrasalmidae family, peaceful herbivore status, 15 cm adult size, blackwater habitat preference, minimum group of 5–6, 300 L+ tank requirement, and jump risk (tight lid essential). Also confirmed species confusion with M. lippincottianus.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Silver Dollar Species Profile
additional.notes: Additional confirmation of diet (strict herbivore, plant tank incompatible), water parameters (pH 6.0–7.5, temperature 24–28°C), shoaling requirement, and breeding overview (egg scatterer, 2000 eggs, ~3-day hatch). Lifespan noted as up to 10 years in good conditions.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers requiring a dedicated large breeding tank with soft, acidic water. Spawning is triggered by seasonal conditioning and slight temperature rise. Up to 2,000 eggs; hatch in ~3 days. Remove adults post-spawn. Fry start on infusoria, then micro-worms and baby brine shrimp.
